players.name: Jacob Widell Zetterström
players.bio: Shot-stopper at Derby County making his World Cup debut at 27. Only 3 caps but earned his place between the sticks with commanding performances. Sweden's new guardian in North America.
players.facts: Plays for Derby County — a club with more famous managers than trophies, but Zetterström keeps things tidy at the back.

players.name: Gustaf Lagerbielke
players.bio: Composed centre-back now plying his trade in Portugal with Braga. Lagerbielke has chipped in with 2 goals from defence across 11 caps — not bad for a man paid to stop them.
players.facts: Born in 2000, Lagerbielke is one of Sweden's millennial defenders — he was still in primary school when Zlatan was in his prime.

players.name: Victor Lindelöf
players.bio: A Vesteras-born rock at the back, Lindelöf has been Sweden's defensive cornerstone for a decade. With 76 caps and now at Aston Villa, this veteran brings Premier League pedigree to the Blågult backline.
players.facts: Lindelöf is nicknamed 'The Iceman' — calm under pressure, rarely ruffled, and seemingly immune to panic.

players.name: Isak Hien
players.bio: The Atalanta defender has become a key figure in one of Serie A's most exciting backlines. Physical, composed, and dominant in the air, Hien arrives at his first World Cup with 29 caps under his belt.
players.facts: Hien was born in Sweden but has Guinean heritage — his journey to Serie A and the World Cup is one of football's better underdog stories.

players.name: Gabriel Gudmundsson
players.bio: Versatile left-sided defender who brings energy and reliability to Sweden's flank. Now at Leeds United, Gudmundsson has earned 24 caps and is a trusted option going forward too.
players.facts: Gudmundsson is one of several Swedes with Icelandic-style surnames — the 'son' suffix is very much on trend in this squad.

players.name: Herman Johansson
players.bio: A solid defensive option with only 3 caps, Johansson is the veteran journeyman who fetched up in MLS with FC Dallas. Dependable and experienced, he's Sweden's emergency cover made good.
players.facts: Herman Johansson plays in Texas — making him possibly the only Swede at this World Cup who already lives on home (ish) turf.

players.name: Lucas Bergvall
players.bio: Born in 2006, Bergvall is the prodigy who made Tottenham sit up and sign him as a teenager. Silky in possession with a football brain well beyond his years, he's Sweden's most exciting young talent.
players.facts: Lucas Bergvall was literally born after Sweden's 2006 World Cup campaign — he's not just the future, he's already the present.

players.name: Daniel Svensson
players.bio: A young, attack-minded defender at Borussia Dortmund, Svensson brings Bundesliga experience to Sweden's backline at just 24. Technically assured and still developing, this is a huge stage for him.
players.facts: Svensson plays for BVB — a club whose fans make more noise than most national teams. Good preparation for a World Cup.

players.name: Alexander Isak
players.bio: Sweden's star striker and one of world football's most lethal forwards. Tall, technical, and frighteningly quick, Isak at Liverpool has been among the Premier League's elite. Sweden's golden boy in 2026.
players.facts: Isak's long, loping stride makes him look effortless — defenders say it's the most deceptive thing about him. They can never catch him.

players.name: Benjamin Nygren
players.bio: Creative midfielder who found his groove at Celtic in Scotland. Nygren has 3 goals in 11 caps and adds flair and invention to the Swedish engine room — a player who loves to make things happen.
players.facts: Nygren is one of several Swedes who've thrived in Scottish football — apparently Scandinavian winters are excellent preparation for Glasgow.

players.name: Anthony Elanga
players.bio: Explosive winger born in Gothenburg with French roots, Elanga left Manchester United for Newcastle and hasn't looked back. Blistering pace, direct running, and 6 international goals make him a constant threat.
players.facts: Elanga's father Robert played for Cameroon — making their family one of football's most internationally divided, in the best possible way.

players.name: Viktor Johansson
players.bio: Reliable second-choice keeper at Stoke City with 12 caps, Johansson is the calm backup Sweden hopes never to need — but is very glad to have. Tall, confident, and a solid shot-stopper.
players.facts: Viktor Johansson was born in Sweden but holds dual Swedish-American nationality. A true transatlantic keeper for a transatlantic tournament.

players.name: Ken Sema
players.bio: The DR Congo-born winger who chose Sweden, Sema has been a dependable servant across 33 caps. Industrious, direct, and still contributing at 32, he's one of the squad's more seasoned wide operators.
players.facts: Ken Sema plays in Cyprus with Pafos — proving that a World Cup squad spot can be earned from almost anywhere on the planet.

players.name: Hjalmar Ekdal
players.bio: Son of former Sweden international Albin Ekdal, Hjalmar is making the family name his own. The Burnley centre-back is solid, composed, and arrives at his first World Cup eager to step out of dad's shadow.
players.facts: His father Albin played at the 2018 World Cup for Sweden. Now it's Hjalmar's turn — football really is a family business in the Ekdal household.

players.name: Carl Starfelt
players.bio: A Champions League winner with Celtic in 2022, Starfelt is the experienced Stockholm-born stopper now patrolling La Liga with Celta Vigo. Calm, dominant, and a leader in the making for Blågult.
players.facts: Starfelt won the Scottish Premiership title with Celtic before moving to Spain — a man who clearly enjoys collecting medals in different countries.

players.name: Jesper Karlström
players.bio: The defensive midfield engine who keeps Sweden ticking, Karlström is the unsung hero of the Blågult setup. Udinese's metronome brings 25 caps of grit, interceptions, and quiet excellence to the squad.
players.facts: Karlström is one of the least glamorous but most essential players in the squad — every great team needs a man who does the dirty work.

players.name: Viktor Gyökeres
players.bio: The most prolific striker in Europe last season, Gyökeres took Sporting CP by storm before Arsenal came calling. Twenty international goals in 33 caps — he and Isak form one of the World Cup's deadliest strike partnerships.
players.facts: Gyökeres scored 54 goals in all competitions for Sporting in 2023-24. That's not a typo. Fifty. Four. Goals.

players.name: Yasin Ayari
players.bio: Solna-born central midfielder who burst onto the scene as a teenager. Now a Brighton regular, Ayari's technical quality and composure beyond his 22 years make him one of Sweden's most exciting midfield prospects.
players.facts: Ayari was born in 2003 — making him one of the youngest players at the tournament. His ceiling is genuinely hard to calculate.

players.name: Mattias Svanberg
players.bio: The Malmö-born midfielder brings energy and box-to-box quality to Sweden's engine room. A Wolfsburg regular with 41 caps, Svanberg is one of the most experienced midfielders in this squad and a reliable workhorse.
players.facts: Svanberg has played in Italy (Bologna) and Germany (Wolfsburg) — a true European midfielder who's done his homework across the continent.

players.name: Eric Smith
players.bio: Halmstad-born centre-back now experiencing life in the Bundesliga 2 with FC St. Pauli. With just 2 caps, this World Cup is a remarkable opportunity for Smith — a player with the most common name in football.
players.facts: Eric Smith is possibly the most ordinarily named player at the entire World Cup 2026. Among 26 Swedes, he stands out by not standing out.

players.name: Alexander Bernhardsson
players.bio: The Göteborg-born right winger brings pace and directness to Sweden's attack from Holstein Kiel. With 11 caps, Bernhardsson is still building his international reputation but has shown plenty of promise on the flank.
players.facts: Bernhardsson plays for Holstein Kiel — a club that made a fairytale run to the Bundesliga. He knows a thing or two about punching above your weight.

players.name: Besfort Zeneli
players.bio: Born in Säter with Kosovar roots, Zeneli is one of Sweden's brightest young midfield talents. Technically gifted and combative, the Union Saint-Gilloise man brings energy and quality to Blågult's midfield options.
players.facts: Zeneli was born in tiny Säter, Sweden — a town of 10,000 people. The World Cup is a somewhat bigger stage than he's used to at home.

players.name: Kristoffer Nordfeldt
players.bio: At 36, Nordfeldt is Sweden's oldest player and a true veteran of the goalkeeping trade. Back at boyhood club AIK, the Stockholm-born stopper provides priceless experience and dressing room wisdom in North America.
players.facts: Nordfeldt has waited years for a World Cup call-up. At 36, he's finally here — proof that patience is a goalkeeper's greatest virtue.

players.name: Elliot Stroud
players.bio: The Uddevalla-born winger is the wildcard of the squad — just 1 cap and plying his trade in the Swedish top flight with Mjällby AIF. A domestic dark horse, Stroud is Sweden's most unexpected World Cup participant.
players.facts: Stroud plays for Mjällby AIF — making him the only player at World Cup 2026 from the Swedish Allsvenskan. Quite the promotion.

players.name: Gustaf Nilsson
players.bio: Tall, powerful centre-forward who has carved out a fine career at Club Brugge in Belgium. With 4 goals in 10 caps, Nilsson offers Sweden an imposing alternative striking option alongside Isak and Gyökeres.
players.facts: Nilsson is 6ft 4in and plays for Brugge in Belgium. If Sweden need a goal from a corner, you know exactly who's getting on the end of it.

players.name: Taha Ali
players.bio: A hometown hero at Malmö FF with just 2 caps, Ali is Sweden's most local wild card — a forward who knows Malmö's streets as well as he knows the penalty box. The World Cup is his biggest stage yet.
players.facts: Ali plays for Malmö FF — the club that gave the world Zlatan Ibrahimović. No pressure, Taha. Absolutely none whatsoever.
